title: Vanity Fair
description: Vanity Fair — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "名利场" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Ambition
memes.description: The driving force of Vanity Fair's plot — the restless desire for wealth, status, and social elevation that propels characters upward and, inevitably, downward.

memes.label: Amelia Sedley
memes.description: The novel's sentimental heroine — a gentle, passive young woman whose unwavering love for George Osborne and eventual marriage to William Dobbin provide the novel's moral counterpoint to Rebecca Sharp's ambition, embodying Thackeray's critique of conventional feminine virtue.

memes.label: Baker Street
memes.description: A London street where the great William Pitt once lived; in Vanity Fair, Mr. Osborne bequeaths his Baker Street house to his widow.

memes.label: Bath
memes.description: England's premier Regency spa town, paired with Cheltenham as a destination for fashionable invalids and social climbers — a place of retreat and diminished relevance for characters who have fallen from London's heights.

memes.label: Battle of Waterloo
memes.description: The decisive battle of June 1815 that ended the Napoleonic Wars — in Vanity Fair, the event that kills George Osborne and transforms Amelia Sedley into a widow, while exposing the vanity, cowardice, and occasional courage of the novel's civilian characters caught in its shadow.

memes.label: Bedford Row
memes.description: A London street in Holborn known for solicitors' offices; in Vanity Fair, home to the firm of Higgs & Blatherwick who handle George Osborne's legal affairs.

memes.label: "BEFORE THE CURTAIN"
memes.description: "As the manager of the Performance sits before the curtain on the boards and looks into the Fair, a feeling of profound melancholy comes over him in his survey of the bustling place."

memes.label: Berkeley Square
memes.description: A fashionable square in Mayfair, London, where Frederick Bullock and his wife Maria Osborne maintain a townhouse — the London anchor of their social ambition, complemented by their villa at Roehampton.

memes.label: Bloomsbury
memes.description: A fashionable residential district in Georgian London, Bloomsbury is home to the Sedley family at Russell Square and features prominently in the early chapters of Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Boggley Wollah
memes.description: A remote and lucrative district in British India where Jos Sedley served as Collector, representing the colonial wealth that underpins the novel's social world.

memes.label: Bois de Boulogne
memes.description: A fashionable park on the western edge of Paris where Becky Sharp rides during her and Rawdon's glittering winter of 1815 after the Battle of Waterloo.

memes.label: Bond Street
memes.description: A fashionable London shopping street in the Regency era, Bond Street represents the material consumption and social display that define Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Boulogne
memes.description: A French port town on the Channel coast, Boulogne-sur-Mer serves as a refuge for Vanity Fair's disgraced and diminished characters, hosting a transient English expatriate community where Becky Sharp briefly reigns as social queen.

memes.label: Brighton
memes.description: The fashionable seaside resort on the Sussex coast where honeymooners, invalids, and fortune-hunters converge in the Regency social season.

memes.label: British Army
memes.description: The military institution that structures the lives of George Osborne, William Dobbin, and Rawdon Crawley, providing the novel's framework of regimental loyalty and the backdrop to the Waterloo campaign.

memes.label: Brompton
memes.description: A semi-rural district on the western edge of Regency London, Brompton is the setting for the Sedley family's reduced circumstances and Amelia's widowhood in the latter half of Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Brussels
memes.description: The Belgian city that served as the social and military staging ground for British society on the eve of the Battle of Waterloo.

memes.label: Bute Crawley
memes.description: The Rector of Crawley-cum-Snailby, brother of Sir Pitt Crawley — a jolly, sports-loving clergyman whose worldly ambitions and wife's scheming form part of Thackeray's satire on the rural clergy.

memes.label: Calcutta
memes.description: The capital of British India and seat of the East India Company's administration, where Jos Sedley serves as a civil servant and William Dobbin's regiment is stationed — representing both the source of colonial wealth and a distant refuge from English society.

memes.label: Camberwell
memes.description: A suburban district south of London, home to the Dobbin family in Vanity Fair — a geographical emblem of the quiet, unglamorous, and ultimately vindicated domestic virtue that the novel's architecture of values endorses.

memes.label: Captain Macmurdo
memes.description: A veteran officer of the Life Guards and old comrade of Rawdon Crawley — Macmurdo serves as Rawdon's second in his confrontation with Lord Steyne, representing the bluff military honour that stands in contrast to the corruption of the civilian world.

memes.label: Cavendish Square
memes.description: A fashionable residential square in Marylebone, London, where George Osborne takes his bride Amelia for their honeymoon in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Chancery Lane
memes.description: A narrow street in London's legal district running between Holborn and Fleet Street, Chancery Lane in Vanity Fair is associated with debt, imprisonment, and the sponging-houses where gentlemen are confined before being committed to the Marshalsea or Fleet Prison.

memes.label: Character Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all characters in Vanity Fair, sorted alphabetically by name. Includes quality ratings and reference counts for each character page.

memes.label: Charity
memes.description: The Christian virtue of selfless giving, which Thackeray exposes as a social performance — genuine in rare cases, but more often a mask for vanity, social climbing, or self-congratulation.

memes.label: Chatham
memes.description: The Kentish military depot town where George Osborne's regiment is stationed before embarking for the Waterloo campaign.

memes.label: Cheltenham
memes.description: A fashionable Regency spa town in Gloucestershire, frequented by Miss Crawley and other characters seeking the medicinal waters, social diversion, and a respectable retreat from London society.

memes.label: Chiswick
memes.description: A Thames-side suburb west of London, Chiswick is home to Miss Pinkerton's academy for young ladies, the setting of Vanity Fair's opening chapter and the place where Amelia Sedley and Rebecca Sharp begin their intertwined stories.

memes.label: Chiswick Mall
memes.description: The site of Miss Pinkerton's academy for young ladies on Chiswick Mall, where Amelia Sedley and Rebecca Sharp begin their entwined stories.

memes.label: Church of England
memes.description: The established national church of England, whose clergy — from ambitious rectors to sycophantic chaplains — populate the novel's satirical portrait of institutional religion.

memes.label: City of London
memes.description: The City of London — the ancient square mile of finance and trade — is the economic engine of Vanity Fair, where the Osborne and Sedley fortunes are made and unmade, and whose commercial judgment serves as the novel's ultimate standard of worldly worth.

memes.label: Concept Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all thematic concepts in Vanity Fair — from fortune and vanity to marriage and reputation. Each concept has a dedicated page analysing its role in the novel.

memes.label: Conduit Street
memes.description: A London street known for its tailoring establishments, Conduit Street in Vanity Fair is personified by Mr. Woolsey the military tailor, whose shop is the setting for Major Dobbin's transformation from awkward soldier to presentable suitor.

memes.label: Covent Garden
memes.description: London's famous market district, mentioned in the novel in connection with the gallant gestures and theatrical associations of Regency society life.

memes.label: The Crawley Family
memes.description: The ancient but shabby-genteel Crawley family of Queen's Crawley, whose members compete fiercely for the fortune of the wealthy spinster Miss Crawley.

memes.label: Cursitor Street
memes.description: A London street off Chancery Lane, notorious in Vanity Fair as the location of Mr. Moss's sponging-house where debtors including Rawdon Crawley were held.

memes.label: Curzon Street
memes.description: A fashionable street in May Fair, London, where Rawdon and Rebecca Crawley reside at No. 201 — the stage of their social ascent and the infamous "Curzon Street catastrophe."

memes.label: Death
memes.description: Death in Vanity Fair reshapes inheritances, alters marriages, and exposes the true characters of survivors — treated with Thackeray's characteristic mixture of sentiment and satire.

memes.label: Debt
memes.description: Debt as the shadow economy of Vanity Fair — every character lives on credit, and financial obligation drives the novel's moral and social machinery.

memes.label: Drury Lane
memes.description: A street in London's Covent Garden district, famous for the Theatre Royal, referenced in Vanity Fair as a cultural landmark of the Regency theatre world.

memes.label: Duchess of Richmond's Ball
memes.description: The famous ball held in Brussels on 15 June 1815, three days before the Battle of Waterloo — in Vanity Fair, the glittering social occasion that is shattered by the distant sound of cannon, exposing the vanity and fragility of the civilian world on the eve of catastrophe.

memes.label: Duke of Wellington
memes.description: Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington (1769–1852), the British commander at the Battle of Waterloo, whose military reputation looms over the Brussels chapters of Vanity Fair — an offstage presence whose strategic genius is assumed rather than demonstrated, serving Thackeray's satire of civilian vanity against the backdrop of great historical events.

memes.label: Dumdum
memes.description: A British East India Company garrison town in Bengal, Dumdum appears in the novel's backstory as the Indian posting where Joseph Sedley encounters Miss Cutler and where conversations about colonial life colour the London domestic scenes.

memes.label: East India Company
memes.description: The powerful trading corporation that governed British India and provided Jos Sedley with his lucrative post as Collector of Boggley Wollah.
aliases: the East India Company's Civil Service

memes.label: Education
memes.description: Education in Vanity Fair is primarily ornamental — a system designed to produce marriageable young women rather than genuinely learned individuals, subjected to Thackeray's sharpest satire.

memes.label: Family
memes.description: Family as both an emotional unit and an economic enterprise — the primary institution through which wealth, status, and identity are transmitted in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Finchley Common
memes.description: A common near London, known in Vanity Fair primarily as the titular subject of Lady Emily Hornblower's satirical religious tract "The Washerwoman of Finchley Common."

memes.label: Flanders
memes.description: The historical region of Flanders in present-day Belgium served as the theatre of British military operations during the Waterloo campaign of 1815, and as transit country for the novel's characters en route from Ostend to Brussels and the frontier.

memes.label: Fleet Street
memes.description: A historic London street where George Osborne briefly stops to purchase a shirt-pin before visiting Amelia Sedley at Russell Square.

memes.label: Fortune
memes.description: The driving force of social mobility in Vanity Fair, where inherited and acquired wealth determines character destiny more decisively than virtue or merit.

memes.label: Frederick Bullock
memes.description: Junior partner at Hulker, Bullock & Co., bankers of Lombard Street, who marries Maria Osborne for her money and embodies the calculating spirit of the City.

memes.label: Fulham
memes.description: A suburban district west of London, where the Sedley family retires after their bankruptcy and where George Osborne and Amelia Sedley are quietly married at a chapel near the Fulham Road.

memes.label: Gambling
memes.description: Gambling pervades Vanity Fair as both a literal pastime and a metaphor for the risks of social life — Rawdon Crawley lives by the card table, and every character gambles with reputation and fortune.

memes.label: Gaunt House
memes.description: Lord Steyne's magnificent London palace in Gaunt Square, a smoky-coroneted symbol of aristocratic power and corruption where Becky Sharp reaches the apex of her social ascent.

memes.label: Gaunt Square
memes.description: A grand London square where Lord Steyne's town palace stands, adjacent to Great Gaunt Street and Gaunt House.

memes.label: General Tufto
memes.description: General Sir George Tufto, commander of the cavalry division in the Waterloo campaign — a middle-aged Peninsular War veteran whose attentions to Rebecca Sharp in Brighton and Brussels illustrate the novel's satire of regency military society, where gallantry on the battlefield blurs indistinguishably into gallantry in the drawing-room.

memes.label: Genteel Society
memes.description: The performance of refined respectability in Regency society, satirised by Thackeray as a hollow marker of social climbing and moral pretension.

memes.label: Gentleman
memes.description: A contested social identity in Vanity Fair, where the gentleman is defined not by birth or manners but by conduct — and Thackeray refuses to provide any character who fully satisfies the definition.

memes.label: George Osborne
memes.description: The novel's nominal hero who possesses charm, good looks, and rank while lacking every quality of a true gentleman.

memes.label: George Osborne's Funeral
memes.description: The funeral of George Osborne after Waterloo — a social ritual that exposes the vanity, hypocrisy, and genuine grief entangled in the novel's treatment of death.

memes.label: Georgy Osborne
memes.description: Georgy Osborne — the young George — is the novel's representative of the next generation, the innocent heir around whom the novel's themes of inheritance and continuity crystallise.

memes.label: Ghent
memes.description: The Belgian city that served as refuge for Louis XVIII's court-in-exile and the destination of Jos Sedley's panicked flight from Brussels on the eve of Waterloo.

memes.label: Governess
memes.description: The liminal social position of the governess in Regency England, used by Thackeray to expose the precariousness of women who must work for a living while maintaining the appearance of gentility.

memes.label: Gray's Inn
memes.description: One of the four Inns of Court in London, where lawyers practise and legal business is transacted — in Vanity Fair, a place of professional consequence where characters learn their fates.

memes.label: Great Gaunt Street
memes.description: The London residence of the Crawley family, Great Gaunt Street near Gaunt Square is the townhouse through which Sir Pitt Crawley asserts his baronetcy's place in fashionable society.

memes.label: Greenwich
memes.description: A riverside town downstream from London on the Thames, Greenwich appears in Vanity Fair as a destination for pleasure outings and whitebait dinners, emblematic of the social rituals of Regency leisure.

memes.label: Hampshire
memes.description: The southern English county that is home to Queen's Crawley and the rural world of the Crawley family, contrasting the values of country gentry with London society.

memes.label: Hanover Square
memes.description: St. George's Church in Hanover Square was the most fashionable wedding venue of Regency London, and its marriage ceremonies frame several key alliances in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Holborn
memes.description: A district in central London, referenced in Vanity Fair as a metonym for safe middle-class domestic life, a marker of professional identity, and a contrast point against which West End prestige is defined.

memes.label: Honour
memes.description: The code of gentlemanly conduct that Thackeray systematically exposes as a performance concealing self-interest, cowardice, and social calculation.

memes.label: House of Lords
memes.description: The upper house of the British Parliament, whose hereditary members represent the apex of the aristocratic power structure that Thackeray satirizes throughout the novel.

memes.label: Hyde Park
memes.description: London's premier fashionable park — in Vanity Fair, the essential stage for Regency social display, where carriages circle the Ring, dandies parade, and reputations are made or slighted by a salute.

memes.label: Hypocrisy
memes.description: Hypocrisy is the central satirical target of Vanity Fair — from Miss Pinkerton's academy to Lord Steyne's drawing-room, Thackeray exposes the gap between the professed values of Regency society and its actual behaviour, implicating both characters and readers in the universal performance of virtue.

memes.label: Isidor
memes.description: Jos Sedley's Belgian valet, who serves during the Waterloo campaign and is notable for his alarm at the French advance and his comic loyalty to his frightened master.

memes.label: Islington
memes.description: A district north of London, used in Vanity Fair as the supposed location of a tradeswoman's shop — a detail that exposes the social pretensions within the regimental community.

memes.label: Jane Osborne
memes.description: Elder daughter of Mr. Osborne and sister to George and Maria, a lonely spinster who lives in Russell Square caring for her aging father, secretly yearning for connection.

memes.label: Jemima Pinkerton
memes.description: ch01-chapter-i-chiswick-mall

memes.label: John Sedley
memes.description: Redirect to Mr. Sedley (John Sedley, Esquire).

memes.label: Jos Sedley
memes.description: The novel's great comic creation — a fat, vain, cowardly East India Company collector whose pretensions to fashion and romance make him the perpetual object of Thackeray's most affectionate satire.

memes.label: Kensington
memes.description: A western district of Georgian London, Kensington — particularly its royal gardens — provides the pastoral backdrop for courtship, military display, and domestic scenes across the middle chapters of Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Lady Bareacres
memes.description: A haughty English aristocrat whose pride is humbled during the Brussels panic before Waterloo — forced to bargain with the woman she had cut dead, Rebecca Sharp, for the horses needed to flee.

memes.label: Lady Blanche Thistlewood
memes.description: Daughter of Lord and Lady Bareacres who marries Lord Gaunt — a haughty aristocratic beauty whose cold disdain for social climbers like Becky Sharp exemplifies the sterile pride of the old nobility in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Lady Crawley
memes.description: ch07-chapter-vii-crawley-of-queens-crawley

memes.label: Lady Emily
memes.description: Lady Southdown's daughter and Lady Jane's sister — the author of pious tracts whose evangelical zeal provides Thackeray with a vehicle for satirising the intersection of aristocracy and religious enthusiasm.

memes.label: Lady Gaunt
memes.description: Wife of Lord Gaunt and daughter-in-law of the Marquess of Steyne, described as "severe, spotless, and beautiful" — the highest-ranking lady in Vanity Fair society.

memes.label: Lady Jane
memes.description: Pitt Crawley's gentle, pious wife — the daughter of Lord Southdown whose quiet goodness provides a moral counterpoint to the scheming and worldliness that surrounds her in the Crawley family.

memes.label: Lady Southdown
memes.description: An evangelical aristocrat who uses religion as an instrument of domestic tyranny — Thackeray's most pointed satire of the alliance between piety and power.

memes.label: Lady Steyne
memes.description: Wife of the Marquis of Steyne — a trembling, silent, superstitious woman broken by a lifetime of contempt from her husband, she represents the dark underside of aristocratic marriage in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Lambeth
memes.description: A district of south London and the seat of the Archbishop of Canterbury, mentioned in a comic scene in Thackeray's Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Leipzig
memes.description: A Saxon city referenced in the later chapters of Vanity Fair as a stopping-point in Becky Sharp's Continental wanderings after her disgrace in England.

memes.label: Lombard Street
memes.description: The historic banking street in the City of London, home to the firm of Hulker, Bullock & Co. in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: London
memes.description: The great metropolis that serves as the novel's primary social stage — the centre of commerce, fashion, and social ambition where fortunes are made and reputations destroyed.

memes.label: London Locations in Vanity Fair
memes.description: A dynamic index of all London locations featured in Vanity Fair — streets, squares, districts, and institutions that form the novel's metropolitan geography.

memes.label: Long Acre
memes.description: A street in central London, historically associated with coach-building and heraldry, mentioned twice in Thackeray's Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Lord Steyne
memes.description: The novel's darkest portrait of aristocratic corruption — a marquess whose power, wealth, and cynicism make him the most dangerous character in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Ludgate Hill
memes.description: A street in the City of London, known in the Regency period for its India shops and retail trade, appearing in a poignant episode of Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Madras
memes.description: A major British military and administrative post on the Coromandel Coast of India, where Major William Dobbin and the —th regiment are stationed, and a key port of call on the sea route between Calcutta and England.

memes.label: Maria Osborne
memes.description: Daughter of Mr. Osborne and sister of George Osborne — her mercenary marriage to Fred Bullock and eager internalisation of her father's values show how patriarchal tyranny reproduces itself in the daughters it crushes.

memes.label: Marriage
memes.description: The central social transaction in Vanity Fair, treated by Thackeray as a marketplace where fortune, status, and ambition are traded under the guise of romance.

memes.label: May Fair
memes.description: The most fashionable district of Regency London, centered on Curzon Street, where Becky and Rawdon Crawley establish their precarious social foothold.

memes.label: Military Figures and Institutions
memes.description: A dynamic index of all military-related wiki pages in Vanity Fair — soldiers, regiments, battles, and army institutions that feature in the novel's critique of martial honour and social ambition.

memes.label: Miss Briggs
memes.description: Miss Crawley's long-suffering paid companion — a timid, kindly spinster who endures the old lady's tyranny for a salary, representing the precarious position of dependent gentlewomen in Regency society.

memes.label: Miss Crawley
memes.description: The novel's great female patron — a wealthy spinster whose fortune of seventy thousand pounds makes her the object of every scheming relative's ambition, embodying Thackeray's satire of wealth and its corrupting influence.

memes.label: Miss Horrocks
memes.description: The butler's daughter at Queen's Crawley whose gaudy ribbons and ambitions to marry Sir Pitt Crawley make her a satirical portrait of vanity at the lowest rung of the social ladder — she reaches for what Rebecca Sharp achieves, but without the skill.

memes.label: Miss Pinkerton
memes.description: The novel's opening satirical portrait — a tyrannical headmistress whose academy embodies the snobbery, hypocrisy, and petty cruelty of the society Thackeray is about to anatomise.

memes.label: Miss Pinkerton's Academy
memes.description: The genteel academy for young ladies on Chiswick Mall where Amelia Sedley and Rebecca Sharp are educated, presided over by the formidable Miss Pinkerton and her timid sister Jemima.

memes.label: Miss Swartz
memes.description: A wealthy mixed-race heiress from the West Indies and schoolfellow of Amelia Sedley at Miss Pinkerton's academy — her fortune makes her a coveted prize in Vanity Fair's marriage market despite the racial prejudice that Thackeray satirises through her portrayal.

memes.label: Miss Wirt
memes.description: The "raw-boned" governess at Queen's Crawley, companion to the Misses Crawley and a sharp-eyed observer of the romantic intrigues surrounding the Crawley sisters.

memes.label: Money
memes.description: The central currency of Vanity Fair — not merely a plot mechanism but the novel's fundamental measure of human worth, driving every character's calculations and exposing the hypocrisy of a society that pretends to value virtue while rewarding wealth.

memes.label: Mr. Bowls
memes.description: Miss Crawley's large confidential butler, a pompous and dignified servant who faithfully guards the Crawley household and participates in Miss Crawley's domestic intrigues.

memes.label: Mr. Osborne
memes.description: George Osborne's wealthy and tyrannical father — a prosperous City merchant whose obsession with social advancement leads him to disown his son and embody the novel's critique of mercenary respectability.

memes.label: Mr. Sedley
memes.description: Amelia Sedley's father — a prosperous London merchant whose financial ruin during the Napoleonic Wars drives the novel's exploration of social decline and the fragility of middle-class respectability.

memes.label: Mr. Wenham
memes.description: Lord Steyne's lawyer, wit, and confidential man of business — a smooth-tongued parliamentary operator whose bland diplomacy masks the ruthless machinery of aristocratic power.

memes.label: Mrs. Blenkinsop
memes.description: The Sedley family's housekeeper on Russell Square who rises to become a banker's wife — a minor character whose transformation from loyal servant to genteel snob embodies the novel's satire of social climbing and ingratitude.

memes.label: Mrs. Bute Crawley
memes.description: ch11-chapter-xi-arcadian-simplicity

memes.label: Mrs. Firkin
memes.description: Miss Crawley's loyal lady's-maid — a sharp-eyed servant whose devotion to her mistress and rivalry with Miss Briggs provides a downstairs counterpoint to the upstairs scheming for Miss Crawley's fortune.

memes.label: Mrs. O'Dowd
memes.description: The wife of Major O'Dowd — a hearty, comic Irishwoman whose good nature and unshakeable self-confidence provide one of the novel's few examples of genuine, unpretentious goodness.

memes.label: Mrs. Osborne
memes.description: The deceased mother of George and Maria Osborne, whose family fortune forms the basis of the Osborne household wealth.

memes.label: Mrs. Sedley
memes.description: Amelia Sedley's fond but foolish mother — a comfortable, conventional woman of the merchant class whose world collapses with her husband's fortune.

memes.label: Mudbury
memes.description: A modest Hampshire village four miles from Queen's Crawley, Mudbury supplies the Crawley estate with its local characters — tradesmen, doctors, farmers, and fiddlers — and anchors the rural world of Vanity Fair's early and middle chapters.

memes.label: Naples
memes.description: The Italian city of Naples appears in Vanity Fair as the last refuge and final stage of Lord Steyne, and a stopping-point in Becky Sharp's continental wanderings.

memes.label: Napoleon Bonaparte
memes.description: Emperor of the French whose return from Elba and final defeat at Waterloo provides the historical backdrop for the novel's central military chapters — the "great roaring war tempest" that sweeps through the characters' lives.

memes.label: Organization Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all organizations and institutions in Vanity Fair — regiments, schools, firms, and households that structure the novel's social world.

memes.label: The Osborne Family
memes.description: The prosperous London mercantile family headed by the harsh, proud Mr. Osborne, whose social ambitions and unforgiving nature shape the tragic arc of his son George.

memes.label: Ostend
memes.description: A Belgian port city on the North Sea — in Vanity Fair, a gateway for armies and exiles, and in later years the favoured resort of the fugitive Jos Sedley, where he meets his final fate.

memes.label: Oxford Street
memes.description: A major London thoroughfare, once the Tyburn Road, and by the Regency era one of the principal shopping and coaching streets of the West End — in Vanity Fair, it is where Becky Sharp, fleeing Great Gaunt Street at dawn, hails a coach to begin her escape.

memes.label: Pall Mall
memes.description: A prestigious London thoroughfare synonymous with gentlemen's clubs, fashionable promenading, and the masculine social world of Regency high society in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Paris
memes.description: The French capital — in Vanity Fair, the glittering destination of British society during the winter of 1815, where Rebecca Sharp achieves her most spectacular social triumph before the inevitable reckoning.

memes.label: Park Lane
memes.description: The fashionable Park Lane address of Miss Crawley's London establishment, a snug house that serves as headquarters for the Crawley family's inheritance intrigues.

memes.label: Patronage
memes.description: The system of social sponsorship by which the powerful dispense favour to dependents — an economy of obligation that Thackeray presents as the hidden structure of Regency society.

memes.label: Pauline
memes.description: Amelia Sedley's French cook and maid in Brussels during the Waterloo campaign, who consoled her mistress after George Osborne marched to battle.

memes.label: Piccadilly
memes.description: A major London thoroughfare connecting the West End to Hyde Park Corner, Piccadilly is the stage for processions, departures, and returns that mark the characters' changing fortunes in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Pimlico
memes.description: A district of central London, mentioned in Vanity Fair through the satirical character John Pimlico and the yet-to-be-built Pimlico arch.

memes.label: Pitt Crawley
memes.description: Pitt Crawley — the elder son and heir of Sir Pitt — is the novel's study in religious hypocrisy. A sanctimonious MP who speaks constantly of duty and principle while scheming as ruthlessly as any character in the novel, he represents the union of piety and self-interest that Thackeray found particularly nauseating.

memes.label: Place Index
memes.description: A dynamic index of all places in Vanity Fair, from London drawing-rooms to Continental battlefields. Sorted alphabetically with quality ratings and reference counts.

memes.label: Most Referenced Pages
memes.description: A dynamic ranking of the most-referenced pages across the Vanity Fair wiki. Sorted by total backlinks, showing which characters, places, and concepts appear most frequently in other pages.

memes.label: Propriety
memes.description: The unwritten code of correct female conduct in Regency society — a standard Thackeray exposes as both a weapon of social control and a performance that the novel's most sympathetic characters cannot sustain.

memes.label: Pumpernickel
memes.description: The fictional German duchy where Becky Sharp and other characters find themselves in exile or retreat, a comic microcosm of petty court life and reduced circumstances.

memes.label: Queen's Crawley
memes.description: The ancestral country estate of the Crawley family in Hampshire, a decaying but proud mansion that embodies old English gentry in decline.

memes.label: Raggles
memes.description: Former butler to the Crawley family who buys the Curzon Street house and becomes landlord to Rawdon and Rebecca Crawley — his ruin at their hands is one of the novel's bleakest illustrations of how the great exploit the small.

memes.label: Rawdon Crawley
memes.description: A cavalry officer of genuine if limited honour, whose love for Rebecca Sharp is the one authentic emotion of his life.

memes.label: Rebecca Sharp
memes.description: The novel's central anti-heroine — a brilliant, ruthless social climber who uses wit, charm, and manipulation to rise from governess to the fringes of high society, embodying Thackeray's satire of ambition without moral constraint.

memes.label: Regent's Park
memes.description: A royal park in northwest London, featured in Vanity Fair as a fashionable location for carriage drives and outdoor recreation.

memes.label: Reputation
memes.description: Reputation as the currency of social credit in Vanity Fair — gained, lost, and traded through the novel's machinery of scandal and gossip.

memes.label: Richmond
memes.description: A picturesque Thames-side town in Surrey, southwest of London — in Vanity Fair, a suburban retreat for characters seeking escape from the city, a place of temporary residences, seminaries, and riverside leisure.

memes.label: Roehampton
memes.description: A suburban village near Richmond, southwest of London, where the banking families of Vanity Fair maintain their villas — most notably the Bullocks, whose "small villa at Roehampton" is a stage for their anxious social climbing.

memes.label: Rome
memes.description: Rome appears briefly in Vanity Fair as a stop on Becky and Rawdon Crawley's continental tour, where they encounter both high society and the dangers of the Roman climate.

memes.label: Rotten Row
memes.description: A broad bridle path in Hyde Park, London, where fashionable society rode on horseback — mentioned in Vanity Fair as the setting for Rawdon Crawley's delight in Becky Sharp's wit.

memes.label: Russell Square
memes.description: The Bloomsbury residence of the Sedley family — a symbol of prosperous merchant respectability that anchors the novel's geography of social class.

memes.label: Sadler's Wells
memes.description: Sadler's Wells was a historic London theatre known for popular entertainment, used in Vanity Fair as a cultural benchmark marking the sweep of the metropolitan theatrical world.

memes.label: Samuel Johnson
memes.description: The great 18th-century lexicographer and man of letters — invoked in Vanity Fair through Miss Pinkerton's claimed acquaintance and the presentation of his Dictionary to departing students at her academy.

memes.label: Satire and Social Critique
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages exploring Thackeray's satire and social critique — the characters, concepts, and narrative techniques through which Vanity Fair dissects the follies of Regency society.

memes.label: The Sedley Family
memes.description: The prosperous London mercantile family whose financial ruin and subsequent recovery through the next generation traces the novel's arc of fortune's reversals.

memes.label: Sentiment
memes.description: Thackeray's treatment of sentimentality as a destructive force and an object of sustained satire in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Sir Pitt Crawley
memes.description: The satirical portrait of the landed aristocracy in Vanity Fair — a baronet who is dirty, ignorant, and perpetually scheming for money despite his title and estate.

memes.label: Smithfield
memes.description: A historic market area in the City of London, mentioned in Vanity Fair in connection with a former convent.

memes.label: Social Class
memes.description: Social class in Vanity Fair is the invisible architecture that determines characters' fates — Thackeray's satire exposes the absurdity and rigidity of Regency England's class system through the intertwined fortunes of merchants, aristocrats, and social climbers.

memes.label: Social Class in Vanity Fair
memes.description: A dynamic index of wiki pages exploring class hierarchy in Vanity Fair — from the aristocracy and landed gentry to the merchant class and servants, tracing Thackeray's anatomy of Regency society.

memes.label: Social Climbing
memes.description: Social climbing is the central drive of Vanity Fair's protagonist Becky Sharp and a pervasive anxiety throughout Regency society — the attempt to rise above one's birth, and the price of that attempt.

memes.label: Society
memes.description: The stratified world of Regency social life that Thackeray satirises as "Vanity Fair" — a marketplace of reputation where birth, wealth, and appearance determine worth and where every character performs for an audience of rivals.

memes.label: Soho
memes.description: A London district of artists, debtors, and continental immigrants — in Vanity Fair, the shabby-genteel neighbourhood where a character lives and owes money "for a mile round."

memes.label: Southampton
memes.description: A major port city on the south coast of England, serving as a transit hub for characters travelling to and from London, and briefly home to the Sedley family after their financial ruin.

memes.label: Southampton Row
memes.description: A London street running from Holborn towards Russell Square, near the Sedley family mansion in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Speculation
memes.description: Speculation as both a financial activity and a governing metaphor for social climbing and risk-taking in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: St. James's
memes.description: The aristocratic heart of Regency London, St. James's — with its palace, park, and court assemblies — represents the summit of social aspiration in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: St. James's Street
memes.description: A prestigious London street in the St. James's district, home to gentlemen's clubs including the Regent Club where George Osborne and his circle spend their leisure hours in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Surrey
memes.description: An English county south of London, home in Vanity Fair to the West India heiress Miss Swartz's mansion and to the Surrey hounds — representing both the aspirational country life of City wealth and the traditional pursuits of the landed gentry.

memes.label: Temple
memes.description: A historic precinct in London encompassing two Inns of Court (Inner Temple and Middle Temple) and the Temple Bar gateway — in Vanity Fair, a place of legal residence and a city boundary marker.

memes.label: Thames Street
memes.description: A commercial street in the City of London, location of the Osborne counting-house and Sedley's coal business offices in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Tom Eaves
memes.description: A knowing London gossip and social informant who "knows everybody's affairs" — Thackeray's narrative device for revealing the secrets of the great families.

memes.label: Vanity
memes.description: The defining vice of Vanity Fair — excessive pride in one's appearance, accomplishments, or status, which Thackeray presents as the universal motive beneath all social behaviour.

memes.label: Vanity Fair
memes.description: "Vanity Fair" is the novel's governing metaphor, borrowed from John Bunyan's The Pilgrim's Progress, representing a worldly marketplace of vanity, greed, and social climbing where all characters — regardless of virtue — are participants.

memes.label: Vauxhall Gardens
memes.description: London's celebrated pleasure gardens, where Jos Sedley's comic courtship of Rebecca Sharp unfolds during the novel's early chapters.

memes.label: War
memes.description: The Napoleonic Wars form the backdrop of Vanity Fair, with the Battle of Waterloo serving as the novel's pivotal turning point that reshapes the fates of its central characters.

memes.label: The Waterloo Campaign
memes.description: A dynamic index of all wiki pages related to the Battle of Waterloo and its surrounding campaign — the military event that reshapes the lives of George Osborne, Amelia Sedley, William Dobbin, and others in Vanity Fair.

memes.label: Westminster
memes.description: The political and legal heart of London, Westminster appears in Vanity Fair as a symbol of public reputation and institutional power — the world of Westminster Hall where a gentleman's name may be ruined, contrasted with the domestic bridges and streets where private life unfolds.

memes.label: William Dobbin
memes.description: The novel's true gentleman — loyal, generous, and self-effacing — who is perpetually overlooked and undervalued by those he serves.

memes.label: Wimbledon
memes.description: Wimbledon was a Surrey village outside London where wealthy Regency families maintained country retreats, used in Vanity Fair as one of the material status symbols that define the marriage market.
